speaker: interviewer
text: You were back in 1937 in Germany. And we're talking about the changes you have seen since 1935 when you first left. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What were the most impressive changes that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- I do not recall any specifics. The-- generally, it was the feeling of being completely isolated from the-- from the non-Jewish population, except for a few gutsy people who didn't mind the comments of their neighbors that they were still having contact with Jews. Even they would-- and my grandparents' home, from-- through a back door that was not visible to the-- from the street. The-- it was that-- that thorough and-- an exclusion from civic life. And I-- I think it would be very difficult for anybody who has not gone through it to really imagine the-- the kind of feeling. And even for me, it is difficult to describe because it-- there are so many intangibles that we take for granted. And the-- like, I can-- I can walk a street in my neighborhood. And even if I don't know the person, I can say, 'Good morning.' Well, there you-- you couldn't. Because, first of all, one did not say 'good morning' anymore in Germany. You had to say, 'Heil Hitler.' And that I wouldn't say. So just from one-- one kind of communication, you can imagine how thorough the exclusion was.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you have to start saying 'Heil Hitler' instead of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Within--

speaker: interviewer
text: --'good morning'?

speaker: interviewee
text: Within a very short time after they took over. I don't remember how many weeks or months it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Nazis visible at this time when you returned?

speaker: interviewee
text: They always were-- were visible because they-- everybody that was anybody was in uniform. And you-- you couldn't walk the street for five minutes without seeing somebody in some form of official clothing. And if you-- if they were the party members or members of either the SR or the SS, they wore that kind of uniform. If they were Hitler Youth or B-- Bund Deutscher Maedchen, or what it was called, you had that uniform. There-- I'd say-- [LAUGHS] --half the population seemed to be uniformed. But certainly, they would have a-- a swastika pin, even if they were civilian uniforms. So the-- you-- you knew that as a Jew, you were walking among enemies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a chance to meet any of your old school friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but it was very difficult to have, really, any meaningful communication with them. Even if they were friendly, you had to be reserved because you were aware that by their being seen with me, they could get into trouble. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- the psychology was so cleverly arranged that any Jew who had any kind of sense of-- of decency would not put anybody into the trouble of being seen in a friendly relationship with a Jew. Were you ever fearful that you might not get out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly. Always. And especially during the time when they were holding onto my passport. I assure you that I did not breathe easily until after the train had crossed the German border. It was almost like breathing a different kind of air.

speaker: interviewer
text: The rest of the four boys that were with you, did they also return at the same time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at the same time. But eventually they all became citizens.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when you went back to the United States, the four of you talked about the changes in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, certainly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think then? What were the conversations different from 1935?

speaker: interviewee
text: The difference was that we had come to the conclusion that our return-trip tickets of 1935 were not for the purpose of permanent return. We-- as long as we held onto those return-trip tickets and as long as we had only a student visa, we knew that our stay in the United States was, at least theoretically, temporary. Even after we-- in our mind, we had made the decision that we wanted to become citizens, it didn't become a reality until that second trip.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you apply for citizenship immediately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Apply for citizenship immediately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes. I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went through all this-- you know, there-- there were two stages. I don't remember the details-- of first papers, which you can take out immediately. And then you-- to-- you take out second papers. And you start a process of-- of-- you had to go to-- to a citizenship school, had to take classes, study about American history, et cetera, et cetera. And they-- I was not-- you couldn't do that until five years if you are on your own, three years if you marry an American citizen. I married an American citizen within the-- in 1940. So I was able to immediately apply for citizenship since I had been there the-- the necessary three years. And I became a citizen in, I think, May of 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back again. When you returned to the United States, what were the questions of the teachers at the seminary? What did they want to know?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they all wanted to know exactly what-- what you've been asking. What-- what did you see? What was your experience? How have things changed in the two years that you were here?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- you feel? Were you ever scared? Were you ever in danger? These were the kind of questions we had to answer. And how was your communication with your parents from then on, from your return?

speaker: interviewee
text: My way of communication had not-- had not changed. The-- and I don't think that there ever was any more-- there-- there just were fewer communications by airmail because the Hindenburg had blown up. So the method of communication remained the same until the fall of 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before that, were you aware of Kristallnacht? You were talking about Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes. They-- we found out about that through the news immediately. And also, I received a cablegram saying that my father had been taken to a-- oh, what's the concentration camp in Bavaria?

speaker: interviewer
text: Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dachau. And I immediately [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tried to do anything I could to-- to get-- to exert influence to get him out. I remember that the highest authority I was able to get access to was the mayor of Cincinnati. And he tried, through the State Department, to make some-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some statement to-- to Germany to-- to get him out. And actually, he was kept there for, I think, a month or two and then was let out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to them, to you parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] They-- the process of isolation continued. Now that was 1938. They couldn't go to synagogue anymore because the synagogue was destroyed. They were just more isolated and probably had the-- the business shrunk to practically nothing. And that went on for almost two years after Kristallnacht. And in the fall of 1940, all the Jews of the-- of the entire state were rounded up on a moment's notice. And being able to take practically nothing were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] put on trains. But they were relatively civilized. The locked trains were-- were passenger trains. And they were on the train for some 40 or 48 hours. And they were dumped into the camp in Gurs, G-U-R-S, in the Pyrenees Mountains. A-- a terrible place which had originally been built for the refugees from the Spanish Civil War, and a place that had daily rain and where they-- they had to wade through mud most of the time. But there they-- they were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] almost abandoned, with-- with very little access to-- to food. But it was not a concentration camp. They were able to get out of the camp to-- for errands and so on. So they-- and the most important thing, they were able to receive mail, both letters and packages, which we-- packages we sent constantly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, we doubled our efforts to get visa and-- and immigration for them. Being a citizen by that time, I was able to give the visa-- give the-- the-- what do you call it? The guarantee for the visa, which meant that they would have a much higher priority than if a stranger had-- alone had given it. I couldn't give it alone because my income wasn't sufficient. But I gave it together with a family of good friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they were able to get-- my parents and my grandmother were able to pick up their visa at the American consulate in Marseilles on December 2, 1941. And five days later, America entered the war and the consulates were closed. So it was really by the skin of their teeth that these three people got out. And of the transport that they were on to Gurs, these three people are the only ones who survived the Holocaust. Everybody else who had not already died in Gurs, as my grandfather did, were sent to-- again, on trains, all the way across Europe to Poland, to Auschwitz or one of the other extermination camps. Everyone who had been on their transport was killed, except for these three people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your aunt-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] your aunt was also [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: She had come over earlier. My aunt came, I believe, in 1938 or early '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got married before your parents came or after your parents--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before your parents came?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were married in 1940. And that speeded my becoming a citizen. And that enabled me to sign the-- the application for the visa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us how you met your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife because of a terrible flood in Cincinnati, which, in 1937, destroyed the water supply for the entire city of Cincinnati. And all institutions had to close, including the Hebrew Union College. And all the students were sent home. But I couldn't be sent home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a classmate of mine was-- had relatives in Dayton. So he called and asked, can I come while the college is closed and bring one of my German classmates with me? And they said yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I spent that forced vacation from the Hebrew Union College in Dayton. And in the course of time, met Miriam. And the rest is history.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: A friend of mine, who had been brought over from Germany by relatives in Dayton, gave a little party. And Miriam was a friend of-- of his. And that's how I met her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how soon after that did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we met in-- that was actually in-- in-- as I mentioned, in the spring of 1937. And we didn't get married until 1940. It was a-- a long courtship.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the five students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every one of the-- of the five remained, became a citizen, was ordained a rabbi, and played a very positive part in the American rabbinate. One who was a-- a bit sickly and did not do too well, but was not-- not too sick to become a chaplain. And after his chaplaincy, worked mainly as a [? Hallel ?] director. But he died young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Each of the remaining four became very active in-- in the American rabbinate And each of the-- of the four remaining people held a chair office in the National-- in the Central Conference of American Rabbis, including two who became presidents. Now that's a-- an office in an organization that has about-- what is it? About 2,000 or 3,000-- 3,000 members.

speaker: interviewee
text: The presidency is one of the most important offices in American Jewry. And both the oldest of the group, who has also become a very well-read scholar and a writer, Rabbi [? Plaut, ?] and the youngest of the group-- one of the two of us who almost didn't make it. They-- those two became presidents of the Central Conference for two-year terms. And my colleague who lives right near here, Rabbi Kaelter, and I-- he became a corresponding secretary. And I was-- I held the position of treasurer for four years, which is rather unusual because it's-- none of those offices are usually held for more than two years. So we-- all four of us were very successful in the rabbinate. The-- my position here--

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when were you ordained?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was ordained in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cincinnati.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you remain in Cincinnati?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My first position was in Dothan, Alabama, which was a congregation that had been started a few years before, had just almost finished building its synagogue. And I was the first rabbi ever to reside in that community, which was quite an experience as a-- as an immigrant, to come to a town where there were about 20 Jewish families. The congregation eventually had about 50 families. But the remaining 25 or 30 families were scattered over an area in Alabama, Georgia, and Florida, an area about the size of the state of New Jersey. And the rabbi-- the-- many of them came quite regularly to services, at least on the High Holidays. And-- but the rabbi had to travel-- [LAUGHS] --over that area in order to stay in touch with his members so that they would know that they-- they are affiliated. It was a very, very interesting way of not only learning the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] learning the-- the ins and outs of the rabbinate, but also to find out about life in rural America. Because Dothan, a town of about 25,000, 30,000 people, was the center of a farming area, with both cotton and peanuts being the-- the main-- main basis for the-- the industry. It was really the-- the best kind of-- of having to learn, because there was nobody there to-- to help you and to instruct you. The closest rabbi was 120 miles away in Montgomery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you, during these years-- let's say, between 1940 and '45. Were the American rabbis, as a unit, working on behalf of the European Jewry in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. The-- in every community, the-- the activities to try to-- to bring refugees over were, if not led, certainly participated in by the-- by the rabbis. And the-- there was a lot of exchange of information so that we would do the most-- the best possible thing to hasten departure from Germany for Jews. The-- or the movement, as such, also was-- and specifically the Hebrew Union College, together with the Jewish Theological Seminary in New York and what was then still a separate organization, the Jewish Institute of Religion, brought scholars to this country and, thus, saved the lives of many of the outstanding scholars and teachers of Judaism. The best-known among the people who were brought to the Hebrew Union College was Abraham Joshua Heschel, who came first to the Hebrew Union College and later went to the Jewish Theological Seminary, which was a bit more sympathetic to his tradition in Judaism. And he-- he and Miriam and I became good friends during the time that he was at the college. And-- which was my senior year of the college. I-- we married at the-- after my junior year. So the last year, I was-- we were married. And were-- therefore, both of us got to know Dr. Heschel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a contact between the Jewish community through the rabbis with the United States government?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not as such, because the-- there were specific Jewish organizations that were created for that purpose, the best-known of which is the JDC, which did the overseas work. And the HIAS, Hebrew Immigrant Aid Society, which, ever since the mass immigration in the early part of this century, had been the organization that worked with the government concerning immigration matters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know a rabbi by the name of Stephen S. Wise?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, yes. I-- I got to know him personally. He was the founder and the president of what was then still a separate seminary. But Wise was a member of the same rabbinic organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know whether he had any connection to the president of the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was well-known that Wise had very close contact with Franklin Roosevelt. In fact, there was-- [LAUGHS] --nobody that was anybody in the United States that Stephen Wise didn't know or that didn't know Stephen Wise. And Steven Wise could pick up a phone and call just about anybody in the United States, from the-- from the president down to everybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain why there was so little done for the European Jewry through the American Jewry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the question of-- of 'little' and 'much,' I think, is a very subjective judgment. Certainly, for those of us who-- who were saved, everything that needed to be done was done. And I was under the impression that, certainly for German Jewry, who were the first to be impacted by the Nazis, a great deal was done. As far as the-- the crucial problem affecting the mass of-- of Jewry in Eastern Europe, the circumstances had changed to the-- to the point where everything worked against the Jews, both those who were immediately hit by the-- by the Nazis and also for those who had-- who wanted to help. Because you had a-- a-- a country that, at that time, was winning a war making a priority of destroying the Jewish community. And the-- until the Allies were in a position where they could really hurt Germany, which was not until late in the war, governments could do very little to help the Jews. Yes, they could have bombed the-- the rails to Auschwitz and didn't do that. How much that would have helped is also questionable. Because if they didn't kill in Auschwitz, they would have killed someplace else. There was really very little they could do, short of winning the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you move to California?

speaker: interviewee
text: When did I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Move to California?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1946. After five years in Dothan, I was asked to become the regional director, opening the first regional office of the Union of American Hebrew Congregations, which is the headquarters of Reform Judaism for the United States and Canada. The-- for the entire West Coast, with a seat in Los Angeles. And just as I was the first to hold that kind of position as a rabbi in the isolated South, so I was the first to hold that position of regional director in the Far West. And again, I had to construct an office and establish the lines of communication. This time not to individual families scattered over the area of the state of-- size of the state of-- of New Jersey, but over the six states closest to the Pacific Ocean.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going Is stop again for a minute. And we'll change tapes.
